topic: An employee is transferred to a different department
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sterile white walls of the new office felt like a physical assault. Sarah clutched the worn strap of her messenger bag, the leather digging into her shoulder. Every glance from the new team members felt like an evaluation, a silent judgment cataloging her perceived inadequacies. She kept her shoulders hunched, trying to shrink into the beige cubicle, acutely aware of the unfamiliar buzz of conversation around her. When Mark from Accounting approached her, his smile was too wide, too practiced. "So, Sarah, glad to have you on board," he chirped, his tone dripping with an insincerity that made her jaw tighten. Sarah offered a tight, practiced smile that didn't reach her eyes. "Thank you," she managed, her voice sounding unnaturally high. Internally, she cataloged every slight—the way they deliberately avoided eye contact when she spoke, the clipped responses to her suggestions. It was exhausting, this constant performance of compliance in a space that felt entirely alien and unwelcoming.

[story 2]

I spent the first week in the logistics department feeling like an unwelcome intrusion. The air in the warehouse hummed with a relentless, mechanical energy that grated on my nerves. Every instruction from my supervisor, David, was delivered in a tone that suggested I was an imposition, a liability. "Just get this done," he’d grunt, not bothering to look up from his tablet. I watched the other workers move with an easy, practiced coordination I simply couldn't replicate. My hands felt clammy, and I kept my movements overly precise, a defense mechanism against any perceived criticism. When someone bumped into me in the narrow aisle, I flinched visibly, pulling back sharply, the brief contact sending a jolt of irritation through me. I just wanted to disappear into the shadows, to be invisible, because being seen here felt like being exposed to scrutiny I wasn't prepared for.

[story 3]

The transition was a slow erosion of my composure. Every email I sent to the new team was met with silence, or worse, a curt, one-word reply. I found myself double-checking every comma, agonizing over the phrasing, terrified that any minor error would be seized upon as proof of my incompetence. Lunch breaks were spent staring blankly at my lukewarm sandwich, the forced camaraderie of the breakroom feeling like a trap. When my manager, Ms. Evans, asked for an update on a project, her expression was cool and distant. "We'll circle back later," she said, her voice flat. I felt a hot flush creep up my neck, a physical manifestation of the simmering resentment building inside. I wanted to snap back, to challenge the dismissal, but I swallowed the retort, forcing myself to nod and retreat back into the silent, suffocating space of my desk.
